speaker: interviewer
text: April 12, 1996. The survivor is Rachael Rubin, R-U-B-I-N. The interviewer is Fran Starr, S-T-A-R-R. We are in the city of Toronto, Canada. The interview will be conducted in English. April 12, 1996. My name is Fran Starr. I will be interviewing Rachael Rubin. We are in the city of Toronto, Canada. And I will be conducting the interview in English. Would you tell us your full name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachael Rubin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-U-B-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schmutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-C-H-M-U-T-T-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1920, October the 4th.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Korzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chuna and Pesa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: One sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Luba.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He had a store. But I don't remember my father. He died when I was small.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when he died?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year and a half or two years, approximately. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything about that time? Do you remember how your father's death affected your life and the life of the family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. My mother was a widow. And later, I understood because she was always sad. And then we lived with our grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live with your grandmother after your father had died?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother do to earn a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother had a restaurant. So my mother used to help my grandmother. And she had other business with cor-- with corn, what she used to buy and sell from the p-- peasant? Peasant?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, from the peasants.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And we-- she made a living for us. Mostly, she was busy, so my grandmother used to take care on us. She used to--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ita Leah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you remember about your grandmother? What type of woman was she?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. She was very nice till, till the end. She was-- with young people, she was young. With older, she was old. And then practical, really, just naturally. And she was taking care on the house and everything, and the restaurant, too. She helped a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most about your grandmother's character? Did she play with you? Did she sing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But she was a practical type. She was-- she knew everything in the garden. We had a big garden. So she used to raise-- we had everything there. And she used to take care in the garden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she teach you how to garden?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too small at the time. But she was very nice. She was more than like a mother. You know, used to bake and cook and you know-- very practical, very-- and we lived together for so many years, till the end of the war-- I mean, till the German came. And she died before Pesach, before the slaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: During your growing-up years, while your mother was away working and your grandmother was looking after you and your sister, what type of activities did you do as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school, actually. In the vacations I used to be occupied with all kind craft things, you know, anything, crochet, sewing things, you know. In the vacation, everything, the libraries.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of books did you like to read?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mysteries. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. And that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about your school. Did you did you go to the public school in Korzec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Public school, yes. Polish public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that a mixed school of boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was good in school, no problems. But I, I liked the Hebrew school better. Some of my friends went to Hebrew school. But Hebrew school, it was expensive. We had to pay more. And my mother couldn't afford. So I, I finished public school in 1934, I think, '34, '35.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there are a lot of Jewish children going to the public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would have been the population of Jewish people in Korzec?

speaker: interviewer
text: And a total population of how many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a mixed population, was Polish and Ukraine, mostly. So I don't remember how many. It wasn't a big town. It was a small.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Jewish people and Gentiles get along well?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, yes, when I was young. We had neighbors, very good neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you friends with Gentile people at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah. It was a mixture, you know. We didn't know the difference at that time. And they didn't know the difference, that we are Jews in there, those years when I went to school. But later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later the best neighbors, they became enemies, you know, just during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was your school from your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Walking distance. I mean, at that time, we walked-- 10, 15 minutes, 20 minutes walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you walked with your sister to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Sometimes with my sister. She, she was-- she was in older grades, you know? Sometimes it was-- I don't remember exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the way your family celebrated the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sad because my father wasn't around. My grandfather wasn't around. And during the holiday, especially, you see the lack of, of males in the family, especially during the holidays. So it was very sad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother had a hard life, you know, by working and supporting us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a typical Shabbat in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Friday night, it was-- actually, Friday afternoon we finished because the front room, it was the restaurant. So Friday afternoon, we closed up the doors. My grandmother used to do it. And because nobody should come in. It was finished for the Shabbat. Lighting candles, having supper. My grandmother used to bake and cook before for Shabbos. And then Saturday morning, we didn't light, like an oven. That she took out from ready-- it was ready warm. It's a big stove. It's not like today. And she took out ready the milk, the warm milk, and everything, and cookies and challah. So we had our breakfast. And we went to shul with my grandmother. And then after lunch, you know, usually, they took a rest, my grandmother and my mother. And we went to play outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES] What type of games did you play outside?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember games. We were talking. We were, you know-- we didn't have, ga-- like, toys or-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would have been your activities?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was sick, I remember, so my mother gave me her jewelry to play in the bath with her jewelry with the chain. Yeah. That's what our toys were.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got together with your friends and went out to play, what would you do? Would you skip? Would you do sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of activity?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wintertime, I remember, we were sl-- it was like a hill-- when the snow was-- you know, wintertime. So we had to slide down and then to walk up and to slide again. That was our enjoyment. Summertime, we did our-- yeah, we had a river between our pl-- place where we lived and the middle of the town, like the center of the town. We had the river. And it was a par-- a park with trees there. So we used to go swimming there. That's what the enjoyment was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your dreams as a young girl growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Go to school to learn. Because when I finished the public school, I was anxious to continue. But it wasn't possible. My mother didn't have the money. And I tried, you know-- a lady came, I remember, and I remind myself, a lady came because I went to ORT to the city of Rovno. And I was there for a short time. And my mother called me back because she had to pay too much money for the school and room and-- so I came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she find out that a lady came. And she was teaching sewing. So I went to take a course, too, with her, designing, like, to make patterns. So I went there. And I was doing this. T-- she, she taught me how to do this. Did you enjoy the sewing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Something to do. Something-- I'm not used-- I guess from young age, I wasn't used to sit and not to, to do something. And I enjoyed this because it gave satisfaction when I see something made myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about your childhood, is there any one or two incidences that stand out in your mind?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remind myself. I remember my sister was treated better than I was, for instance, because she was the older one. So before Yom Tov, she used to get new clothes. For me, my mother used to bring the clothes, the old one, to the dressmaker, and to remodel these on the other side. You know, to get for me-- but something new, it should be for Yom Tov. And patent leather shoes, I was always looking at them just-- you know, I was-- I didn't want to wear them often. They shouldn't get dirty. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have aunts and uncles and cousins who you were close with?

speaker: interviewee
text: My cousins. I had cousins, yes. And then-- and the cousins, we were close. I mean, they used to come to my grandmother because they were grandchildren. So they used to come to my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the first signs of anti-Semitism happen to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was in '38 or at the start of '37, '38. It started to-- it wasn't the same, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: What changed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What changed, they used to say that in the government, in the Polish government, German people came in, like, like spies, you know? Even the Polish government didn't know it of them. So and they started to rule already against Jews, not to buy in the stores, not to-- how to say-- slaughter them. It was a shochet, for instance. They, they said that they shouldn't work, shouldn't kill the chicken the way Jews supposed to, you know? And we felt already that it's, it's not the same. Even in school, you know, it's-- they looked at us differently [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] till '39, 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me more specific examples of how you were treated differently? How did your neighbors change? How did people who you associated with daily change?

speaker: interviewee
text: I noticed mostly the-- when, when they came, when, when the war started in Poland. Then, we noticed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But prior to 1939, you indicated that there were some changes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did those changes affect you personally or your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the business. My mother was suffering because, for instance, we had to pay-- what's it-- like income tax, like business tax. We had a business. Then they looked, for instance, on the cleanness, and the restaurant should be perfectly, perfectly clean. Even it was perfect clean, they was-- they were trying to find something, you know. Said it's not correct. It's this and this, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened when they found something that wasn't correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was trouble. So we tried. We tried to-- especially my sister was that time helping in the restaurant. We tried in the evening to wash everything, to make clean, you know, because we knew that maybe they will come in, the inspector, health inspector, like. And he ca-- when he'll come in, he shouldn't find something wrong. So and he paid special attention more on us because it was in the same street another restaurant. They didn't pay as much attention there as they paid on us, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-- because we're Jews. And, and my mother was suffering from this, for instance, to pay the taxes in time. So and sometimes she didn't have the money. So she-- I mean, she was really young. She got gray from all the problems what she had.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the inspectors came in to inspect the restaurant, and if they found something that wasn't right, what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What did they do? They, they could like-- we had to pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Penalty, like, a fine. That's right. We had to pay.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the neighbors react to this sort of treatment of the Jewish people? You know, they still were not bad, I can say because they started when, when really, they had the power, you know. And the German gave them the power in their hand. Then it was the real problems. Before, not. Did your family think about leaving Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the family talk about when there were these early warning signals?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were so attached-- especially my grandmother was so attached to her garden and to her house, you know? That never were thinking about moving. Oh, no. My mother had chances to go like because she was a widow, and she was still young. So they matched her up with people, you know, to go to the United States, or-- But she, she wasn't thinking about leaving her mother, my grandmother. And she didn't want us to have a stepfather. So we were always together. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did life start changing drastically for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started. And Poland was divided because Poland-- the war in Poland wasn't long, just a few weeks, you know? So and Poland was divided. And by us were Russian people. And the other part of Poland were the German. A lot of people from Poland came to us because they suffered already from the Germans. They came, and they warned us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they warn you? What did they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Said-- they said-- they said what's going on in Poland, you know? There are killings. There are all kind of things. But a lot of people from our part, they left to Russia. But we were too much attached with our mother and grandmother that we didn't want to leave them, and they didn't want to go. So we, we stood together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then what happened? It was 18 months the Russians were by us after German started the war with Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians were in power, how was your life? How were you treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't say too bad. OK. Later. I, I was working. I went to school, back, in the evenings to take courses. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You took courses to learn Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian, naturally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of work did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the day, I was working in an office by the Russian. And I was-- I knew a little bit typing and accounting. They taught me, actually. I wasn't a professional accountant, but they taught me how to do this. And I caught it very easy. I was working for them. But it didn't last long. It's 18 months. And then they left. I had a lot of chances to go away. They begged me, friends and neighbors, people from Poland, when they were new, newcomers, like by us. They left right away, and they said to me, come. Don't stay here. But me with my sister didn't want leave. So we stood with my mother and grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the day the Germans came into your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I remember the day. They came, and they made a lot of planes came over our city. And they started to bomb, throwing bombs. We ran into the garden-- me, with my mother and sister and grandmother. And we were under the trees, you know. A lot of bombs were flying and pieces of bombs. They were-- it was throwing on us. They hurt, you know? For a day, we were covered like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then when it was quiet, already, so we came out. And we saw a lot of people dead-- neighbors-- because they were running, you know? They didn't know what to do, and where to cover themselves. So a lot of people, neighbors, were dead, killed. These were Jewish neighbors as well as Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish. Yeah. Yeah. I don't remember exactly, but I remember neighbors, Jewish neighbors, were killed at the time. And that the day-- the first day when they came, they came in. They were afraid to go into a city, to a town, until they know that it's clean, that there is no-- I mean, against them. So they made it clean with the bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there damage to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No damage. But the garden was-- the earth was upside down, you know, because from the pieces of the bomb. And then when they came in, it was awful. Every single day it was-- they collected some people. They send away to work. And the people didn't come back. I at the time was working. They make like a Judenrat. You know what it means? Like a office. And I was there working on a typewriter, too. So the-- once--

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you chosen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why was I chosen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I knew how to work in a office. So the man, what-- he was the manager, you know, called me I should work for him. So I was working. And once a Ukraine guy from the police-- because the Ukraine were right away the managers. They were the, the-- in the police and the officers. And so he came in and, he said that he needs to change a tape in the typewriter. You know, the ta-- it's a old-fashioned typewriter with the black tape and two rolls. He has to change the ty-- the tape. I should come there and change the tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the manager says, OK. Go. That's it. So I went. And it was in the police, police station. I went. And I was waiting, waiting. He didn't give me a tape. He didn't give me a machine to do this. So I was asking, what should I do here? He said come. I'll show you what to do. And he brought me in into a toilet. I should clean the toilet. We'll go to break now.
